overview: Stiflenought is a W/U tempo strategy that leverages Lurrus of the Dream-Den to repeatedly cast cheap spells and recur threats. Stock Up provides card advantage and graveyard fuel, while Phyrexian Dreadnought serves as a resilient finisher. The deck aims to stabilize early, outvalue opponents through recursion, and close games with unblockable or high-toughness creatures.
win_conditions: Lurrus recursion loops with cheap threats
Stock Up card advantage leading to resource exhaustion

keep_criteria: Hands with Lurrus and cheap spells
Hands with Stock Up and early interaction
Keep hands with 2-3 playable lands, Lurrus, Stock Up, or early interaction (Stifle, removal).
Drop hands with 3+ lands and no early plays, or hands consisting solely of removal without card draw or a finisher.
Prioritize hands that can play a land and a spell on turn 1 or 2.
mulligan_criteria: Hands lacking Lurrus or Stock Up
Hands with no early interaction or lands

early_game: Cast Lurrus to recur key spells
Use Stock Up to draw into answers
Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cantrip or hold up interaction depending on matchup.
Turn 2: Cast Lurrus or play a second land.
Sequence lands to ensure blue/white mana for Stifle and Lurrus.
If facing aggro, hold interaction for early threats
against control, establish card advantage with Stock Up or Lurrus.
mid_game: Treat your graveyard as a secondary hand
only cast Dreadnought when you have a plan to recur it or it applies immediate pressure.
Use Stock Up to dig for answers or recursion pieces.
Avoid overcommitting to the board without Lurrus or interaction ready.
Commit threats only when you have interaction or recursion locked in.
Dreadnought is a liability if unprotected or unrecurable.
late_game: Maintain card advantage with Stock Up loops
Control board state until opponent runs out of resources
Phyrexian Dreadnought cast from the graveyard and recurring via Lurrus, or a board presence of Lurrus plus Dreadnought.
Sometimes a Stifle-protected Dreadnought that cannot be removed wins the game outright.
Go all-in when you have graveyard recursion secured, enough mana to cast Dreadnought, and interaction to protect it.
Also commit when the opponent has no removal or graveyard hate in play.

key_cards.name: Lurrus of the Dream-Den
key_cards.notes: Cast repeatedly with cheap spells to fuel Stock Up and recur threats

key_cards.name: Stock Up
key_cards.notes: Cast from hand or graveyard to draw cards and discard into Lurrus targets

key_cards.name: Phyrexian Dreadnought
key_cards.notes: Cast when mana is available or recur with Lurrus to close games

sequencing_tips: Cast Stock Up before Lurrus to fill graveyard
Hold up mana for counterspells before committing to Lurrus
Prioritize removing opponent's early threats before setting up Dreadnought
Common mistake: casting Dreadnought without a plan to recur it or protect it.
Stifle timing is crucial—save it for opponent's removal or key spells, not just for fun.
protect it or have a backup plan.
In an Unknown format, adapt to the local meta but keep the core recursion engine intact.
Always track opponent's graveyard and hand size to avoid walking into sweepers or hate cards.

matchups.advice: Prioritize early removal and Lurrus recursion to stabilize
Disrupt key pieces with counterspells and discard
Race with Dreadnought and maintain card advantage
Use Stifle to protect your own spells or Dreadnought. Cast threats in response to counterspells when possible. Use cantrips to dig around interaction and force them to spend mana on multiple threats.
Prioritize early interaction to disrupt their mana development. Use Stifle to counter key ramp spells or mana dorks. Dreadnought can stabilize by applying pressure while they are still ramping.

Treat it as a resource war. Prioritize graveyard protection, out-value with card draw, and use Stifle to protect your Lurrus and Dreadnought from their recursion. Win by having more interaction and better graveyard management.

common_threats: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Scavenging Ooze, etc.), counterspells, and sweepers.
Note when opponents hold up mana for removal versus interaction.
Track how many cards they have in hand to gauge if they can answer your next threat.
